informal: Let $X$ be a metric space in which every infinite subset has a limit point. Prove that $X$ is separable.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_2_24_corrected
    {X : Type*} [MetricSpace X]
    (hTB : TotallyBounded (Set.univ : Set X)) :
    SeparableSpace X
reasoning: The informal hypothesis is "every infinite subset of X has a limit point" (limit point compactness / Bolzano–Weierstrass property). The Lean signature instead assumes `TotallyBounded (Set.univ : Set X)`. These are not the same hypothesis. In a metric space, limit point compactness implies sequential compactness, hence compactness, hence total boundedness; so the informal hypothesis implies the Lean one. The converse fails: e.g. the open interval (0,1) is totally bounded but the infinite subset {1/n} has no limit point in (0,1). Thus the Lean statement uses a strictly weaker hypothesis to derive the same conclusion (separability), so it logically implies the informal claim but is not implied by it.
